title: Federal Reserve issues FOMC statement
summary: The Federal Open Market Committee voted 11-1 to maintain the federal funds rate target range at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent at its March 18, 2026 meeting. The Committee noted solid economic growth, low job gains, and somewhat elevated inflation while emphasizing elevated uncertainty about the economic outlook, including uncertainty around Middle East developments. Stephen I. Miran was the sole dissenter, preferring a 25 basis point cut.
key_points: Fed maintained the target range for the federal funds rate at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent (3.50%-3.75%)
Vote was 11-1; Stephen I. Miran dissented preferring to lower the target range by 1/4 percentage point
Economic activity assessed as expanding at a solid pace
Job gains remained low; unemployment rate little changed in recent months
Inflation remains somewhat elevated; Committee remains committed to 2% inflation objective
Committee noted elevated uncertainty about economic outlook and flagged Middle East developments as uncertain
why_it_matters: This FOMC decision keeps monetary policy on hold, maintaining restrictive rates while the Fed monitors incoming data. The lone dissent from Miran—who preferred a cut—suggests at least one committee member believes current policy is overly tight. Market participants will focus on whether this represents a pause or the start of a prolonged hold as inflation remains above target.
macro_context: The Fed is navigating a mixed economic backdrop: solid growth but subdued labor markets and above-target inflation. The explicit mention of Middle East uncertainty adds geopolitical risk to the policy calculus. Forward guidance remains data-dependent with no clear signal on the timing of future adjustments.
important_numbers: 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent (target federal funds rate range)
notes: Source is a primary official FOMC statement from the Federal Reserve. Published March 18, 2026, within 90 days of TODAY (2026-03-31), making it a current reference. Vote tally of 11-1 with a dissent from Miran preferring a cut is notable. No changes to balance sheet policy or forward guidance language noted.
